The ''kofun'' was designated a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1922, with the area under protection extended in 2010 and 2012<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/424|title=吉田古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 May 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Yoshida Kofun was initially believed to be a square-type {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}. The tumulus has a height of about 1.6 meters and is roughly of 26 meters on each side; however, per a survey conducted in 2006, it is now believed that the tumulus was a highly unusual octagonal-type {{nihongo|''{{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=hakkaku-fun|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun}} instead. In 1914 an elementary school teacher attached to the Prefectural Mito Women's Normal School, demolished a part of the tumulus for soil, uncovering the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]], which was found to be decorated with mural paintings depicting armor, swords, quivers, and spears. The burial chamber was three meters long and from 1.32 to 1.46 meters in width. Such decorated tombs have been found in the [[:en:Kansai region|Kansai region]] and in [[Kyushu]]; however, this was the first such tumulus found in the Kantō region. [[:en:Grave goods|Grave goods]] included gold rings, fragments of plate armor made of iron, straight swords, and ''[[magatama]]'' beads. Due to the rarity of decorated ''kofun'', a number of noted archaeologists (including [[:en:Torii Ryūzō|Torii Ryūzō]]) have researched the site over the years and have determined that it dates from the middle of the 7th century AD.
The tumulus is located a five minute walk from the "Yoshida Kofunen" bus stop on the Kanto Railway bus from [[:en:Mito Station (Ibaraki)|Mito Station]] on the [[:en:East Japan Railway Company|East Japan Railway Company]] [[:en:Jōban Line|Jōban Line]].
==See also== {{Portal|History|Japan}} *[[:en:List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ibaraki)|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ibaraki)]]

Due to the rarity of decorated ''kofun'', a number of noted archaeologists (including {{ill|Torii Ryūzō|en|Torii Ryūzō|WD=Q707532|lt=Torii Ryūzō}}) have researched the site over the years and have determined that it dates from the middle of the 7th century AD.
The tumulus is located a five minute walk from the "Yoshida Kofunen" bus stop on the Kanto Railway bus from {{ill|Mito Station (Ibaraki)|en|Mito Station (Ibaraki)|WD=Q909499|lt=Mito Station}} on the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Jōban Line|en|Jōban Line|WD=Q1369263|lt=Jōban Line}}.

{{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Yoshida Kofun'''|吉田古墳|}} is ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Motoyoshida neighborhood of the city of [[Mito, Ibaraki]] in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The ''kofun'' was designated a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1922, with the area under protection extended in 2010 and 2012<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/424|title=吉田古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 May 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Yoshida Kofun was initially believed to be a square-type {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}. The tumulus has a height of about 1.6 meters and is roughly of 26 meters on each side; however, per a survey conducted in 2006, it is now believed that the tumulus was a highly unusual octagonal-type {{nihongo|''{{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=hakkaku-fun|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun}} instead. In 1914 an elementary school teacher attached to the Prefectural Mito Women's Normal School, demolished a part of the tumulus for soil, uncovering the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]], which was found to be decorated with mural paintings depicting armor, swords, quivers, and spears. The burial chamber was three meters long and from 1.32 to 1.46 meters in width. Such decorated tombs have been found in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} and in [[Kyushu]]; however, this was the first such tumulus found in the Kantō region. {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} included gold rings, fragments of plate armor made of iron, straight swords, and ''[[magatama]]'' beads. Due to the rarity of decorated ''kofun'', a number of noted archaeologists (including {{ill|Torii Ryūzō|en|Torii Ryūzō|WD=Q707532|lt=Torii Ryūzō}}) have researched the site over the years and have determined that it dates from the middle of the 7th century AD.
The tumulus is located a five minute walk from the "Yoshida Kofunen" bus stop on the Kanto Railway bus from {{ill|Mito Station (Ibaraki)|en|Mito Station (Ibaraki)|WD=Q909499|lt=Mito Station}} on the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Jōban Line|en|Jōban Line|WD=Q1369263|lt=Jōban Line}}.

Due to the rarity of decorated ''kofun'', a number of noted archaeologists (including {{ill|Torii Ryūzō|en|Torii Ryūzō|WD=Q707532|lt=Torii Ryūzō}}) have researched the site over the years and have determined that it dates from the middle of the 7th century AD.
The tumulus is located a five minute walk from the "Yoshida Kofunen" bus stop on the Kanto Railway bus from {{ill|Mito Station (Ibaraki)|en|Mito Station (Ibaraki)|WD=Q909499|lt=Mito Station}} on the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Jōban Line|en|Jōban Line|WD=Q1369263|lt=Jōban Line}}.

[[User:Immanuelle|Immanuelle]] ([[User talk:Immanuelle|talk]]) 10:33, 11 June 2025 (UTC)}} {{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} | name = Yoshida Kofun | native_name = 吉田古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Yoshida-kofun zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yoshida Kofun | map_type = Japan Ibaraki Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yoshida Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = 10 | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|36.358639|N|140.475167|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Mito, Ibaraki]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} | type = ''[[kofun]]'' | part_of = | length = | width = | area = 2,319.64 sq.m | {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Yoshida Kofun'''|吉田古墳|}} is ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Motoyoshida neighborhood of the city of [[Mito, Ibaraki]] in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. The ''kofun'' was designated a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1922, with the area under protection extended in 2010 and 2012<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/424|title=吉田古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 May 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Yoshida Kofun was initially believed to be a square-type {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}. The tumulus has a height of about 1.6 meters and is roughly of 26 meters on each side; however, per a survey conducted in 2006, it is now believed that the tumulus was a highly unusual octagonal-type {{nihongo|''{{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=hakkaku-fun|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun}} instead. In 1914 an elementary school teacher attached to the Prefectural Mito Women's Normal School, demolished a part of the tumulus for soil, uncovering the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]], which was found to be decorated with mural paintings depicting armor, swords, quivers, and spears. The burial chamber was three meters long and from 1.32 to 1.46 meters in width. Such decorated tombs have been found in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region}} and in [[Kyushu]]; however, this was the first such tumulus found in the Kantō region. {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods}} included gold rings, fragments of plate armor made of iron, straight swords, and ''[[magatama]]'' beads. Due to the rarity of decorated ''kofun'', a number of noted archaeologists (including {{ill|Torii Ryūzō|en|Torii Ryūzō|WD=Q707532|lt=Torii Ryūzō}}) have researched the site over the years and have determined that it dates from the middle of the 7th century AD.
The tumulus is located a five minute walk from the "Yoshida Kofunen" bus stop on the Kanto Railway bus from {{ill|Mito Station (Ibaraki)|en|Mito Station (Ibaraki)|WD=Q909499|lt=Mito Station}} on the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Jōban Line|en|Jōban Line|WD=Q1369263|lt=Jōban Line}}.
==See also== {{Portal|History|Japan}} *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ibaraki)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ibaraki)|WD=Q16248240|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ibaraki)}}

<!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Yoshida Kofun]][[en:Yoshida Kofun]][[ja:吉田古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[co:commons:Category:Yoshida Kofun]][[en:Yoshida Kofun]][[ja:吉田古墳]] <!--interwikis from wikidata-->[[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Yoshida Kofun]][[en:Yoshida Kofun]][[ja:吉田古墳]]
{{Infobox ancient site|type = {{ill|Octagonal Kofun|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}} | name = Yoshida Kofun | native_name = 吉田古墳 | native_name_lang = | image = Yoshida-kofun zenkei.JPG | image_size = | alt = | caption = Yoshida Kofun | map_type = Japan Ibaraki Prefecture#Japan | map_alt = location in Japan | relief = 1 | map_caption = Yoshida Kofun | map_size = 270px | altitude_m = 10 | altitude_ref = | coordinates = {{Coord|36.358639|N|140.475167|E|region:JP_type:landmark|display=inline,title}} | map_dot_label = | location = [[Mito, Ibaraki]], {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | region = {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} | type = ''[[kofun]]'' | part_of = | length = | width = | area = 2,319.64 sq.m | volume = | diameter = | circumference = | height = | built = mid-7th century AD | abandoned = | epochs = [[Kofun period]] | cultures = | discovered = | excavations = 1914, 2006 | archaeologists = | condition = | ownership = | management = | public_access = Yes (no facilities) | Historic Site = | website = | architectural_styles = | architectural_details = | notes = {{box|background=white|align=center|wide=yes|border size=3px|border color=brown|text align=center|[[Monuments of Japan|National Historic Site of Japan]]}} }} The {{nihongo|'''Yoshida Kofun'''|吉田古墳|}} is ''[[kofun]]'' [[Kofun]] located in the Motoyoshida neighborhood of the city of [[Mito, Ibaraki]] in the northern {{ill|Kantō region|en|Kantō region|WD=Q132480|lt=Kantō region|1=Kantō region}} of {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. The ''kofun'' was designated a [[:en:Historic Sites of Japan|National Historic Site]] in 1922, with the area under protection extended in 2010 and 2012<ref name= "Bunka">{{cite web|url=https://kunishitei.bunka.go.jp/heritage/detail/401/424|title=吉田古墳|work=Cultural Heritage Online|publisher=Agency for Cultural Affairs|language=Japanese|accessdate=25 May 2020}}</ref>
==Overview== The Yoshida Kofun was initially believed to be a square-type {{nihongo|''{{ill|Square Kofun|lt=hōfun|ja|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Square Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|方墳|1=Square Kofun|1=Square Kofun}}. The tumulus has a height of about 1.6 meters and is roughly of 26 meters on each side; however, per a survey conducted in 2006, it is now believed that the tumulus was a highly unusual octagonal-type {{nihongo|''{{ill|Octagonal Kofun|lt=hakkaku-fun|ja|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|13=User:Immanuelle/Octagonal Kofun|comment=enwiki is a redirect}}''|八角墳|1=Octagonal Kofun}} instead. In 1914 an elementary school teacher attached to the Prefectural Mito Women's Normal School, demolished a part of the tumulus for soil, uncovering the [[:en:Burial chamber|Burial chamber]], which was found to be decorated with mural paintings depicting armor, swords, quivers, and spears. The burial chamber was three meters long and from 1.32 to 1.46 meters in width. Such decorated tombs have been found in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} and in [[Kyushu]]; however, this was the first such tumulus found in the Kantō region. {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} included gold rings, fragments of plate armor made of iron, straight swords, and ''[[magatama]]'' beads. Due to the rarity of decorated ''kofun'', a number of noted archaeologists (including {{ill|Torii Ryūzō|en|Torii Ryūzō|WD=Q707532|lt=Torii Ryūzō|1=Torii Ryūzō}}) have researched the site over the years and have determined that it dates from the middle of the 7th century AD.
The tumulus is located a five minute walk from the "Yoshida Kofunen" bus stop on the Kanto Railway bus from {{ill|Mito Station (Ibaraki)|en|Mito Station (Ibaraki)|WD=Q909499|lt=Mito Station|1=Mito Station (Ibaraki)}} on the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Jōban Line|en|Jōban Line|WD=Q1369263|lt=Jōban Line|1=Jōban Line}}.
==See also== {{Portal|History|Japan}} *{{ill|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ibaraki)|en|List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ibaraki)|WD=Q16248240|lt=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ibaraki)|1=List of Historic Sites of Japan (Ibaraki)}}

Such decorated tombs have been found in the {{ill|Kansai region|en|Kansai region|WD=Q164256|lt=Kansai region|1=Kansai region}} and in [[Kyushu]]; however, this was the first such tumulus found in the Kantō region. {{ill|Grave goods|en|Grave goods|WD=Q1399692|lt=Grave goods|1=Grave goods}} included gold rings, fragments of plate armor made of iron, straight swords, and ''[[magatama]]'' beads. Due to the rarity of decorated ''kofun'', a number of noted archaeologists (including {{ill|Torii Ryūzō|en|Torii Ryūzō|WD=Q707532|lt=Torii Ryūzō|1=Torii Ryūzō}}) have researched the site over the years and have determined that it dates from the middle of the 7th century AD.
The tumulus is located a five minute walk from the "Yoshida Kofunen" bus stop on the Kanto Railway bus from {{ill|Mito Station (Ibaraki)|en|Mito Station (Ibaraki)|WD=Q909499|lt=Mito Station|1=Mito Station (Ibaraki)}} on the {{ill|East Japan Railway Company|en|East Japan Railway Company|WD=Q499071|lt=East Japan Railway Company|1=East Japan Railway Company}} {{ill|Jōban Line|en|Jōban Line|WD=Q1369263|lt=Jōban Line|1=Jōban Line}}.
